channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Europe Rejects Trump’s Demands for Warships to Reopen the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: While some European countries said they were discussing ways to help reopen the Strait of Hormuz, several rejected President Trump’s calls to send warships.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times

item.title: What Are Trump’s Positions on Iran? They Can Change by the Sentence.
item.description: The president is no stranger to staking out contradictory stands, part of what his aides say is his negotiating style. But on Iran, his shifting positions are colliding with the consequences of war.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump leaving the White House last month. The conflict in Iran has come with consequences including a rising death toll and soaring gas prices.

item.title: What the Iran War Means for the U.S.-China Relationship
item.description: With the Xi-Trump summit almost certainly delayed, and tensions rising over the war in Iran, vital issues for both the U.S. and China are also being cast into uncertainty.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Chinese destroyer Tangshan at dock near an Iranian navy ship in Cape Town, South Africa, in January for joint maritime safety operations.

item.title: As American Bombers Take Off From R.A.F. Fairford in UK, Iraq War Looms Large
item.description: R.A.F. Fairford was the site of repeated antiwar protests during the Iraq war in 2003. Now it is being used again as a base for U.S. bombing missions in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: U.S. Air Force personnel loading munitions into a B-1 bomber at R.A.F. Fairford on Monday.

item.title: Cuba Ready to Accept Outside Investment, Top Official Says
item.description: President Trump’s words came amid a nationwide blackout and as a top Cuban official said his country would move to open the economy to foreign investors.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal, Jack Nicas and Frances Robles
item.media:description: The Güinera neighborhood of Havana on Friday.

item.title: Trump Administration Seeks to Remove Cuba’s President From Power During Negotiations
item.description: The United States has told Cuba that for meaningful progress to be made in negotiations, President Miguel Díaz-Canel must step down, said people familiar with the talks.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles, Edward Wong and Annie Correal
item.media:description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel of Cuba during a visit to Venezuela last year.

item.title: Oil Begins Flowing Through California Pipeline Under Trump Order
item.description: Oil had not flowed through the pipeline since a 2015 rupture caused an environmental disaster on California’s Central Coast. It sets up a new fight between the Trump administration and state officials.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:description: A leaky pipeline was the source of a spill, seen in this 2015 file photo, that polluted Refugio State Beach, north of Goleta, Calif., and killed hundreds of birds and marine mammals. The pipeline’s new owner, Sable Offshore, says the problems have been fixed and that it has restarted the flow of oil at the direction of the Trump administration.

item.title: War Isn’t the Only Thing on Trump’s Mind
item.description: President Trump spoke to journalists for about 40 minutes before holding a news conference on Monday, offering a glimpse into his priorities.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: President Trump on Monday at the White House, where he went on a series of tangents that were notable even for a president who boasts about his oratorical “weave.”

item.title: Judge Strikes Down RFK Jr.’s Vaccine Policies in Blow to Trump’s Health Agenda
item.description: Ruling on a lawsuit brought by several prominent medical organizations, a district court said the federal government had not based its decisions on science in limiting Covid shots and revising the childhood immunization schedule.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: A meeting of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices in Atlanta in December.

item.title: Airport Security Lines Grow as TSA Goes Unpaid in Partial Shutdown
item.description: “There’s going to be a breaking point sooner or later,” one union official warned, with travelers at some airports being told to arrive three hours ahead of time.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:description: With Transportation Security Administration officers missing paychecks as the partial government shutdown enters its fifth week, long lines are beginning to form at major airports.

item.title: Richard Grenell: From Trump Cabinet Hopeful to Ex-Kennedy Center Manager
item.description: Richard Grenell once hoped to be President Trump’s secretary of state. Instead, Mr. Trump just replaced him as Kennedy Center president.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: Richard Grenell, left, the outgoing president of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, was seated next to his replacement, Matt Floca, the center’s vice president of facilities operations, at a White House news conference on Monday.

item.title: Best and Worst Moments From the 2026 Oscars
item.description: There was a lot to take in, from Michael B. Jordan’s thrilling win to the perplexing “bum drum.”
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times

item.title: How ‘Marty Supreme’ and Timothée Chalamet Went Home With No Oscars
item.description: A few years ago, the indie studio A24 was luxuriating in Academy Awards. On Sunday it was shut out entirely.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:description: “Marty Supreme,” directed by Josh Safdie and starring Timothée Chalamet, received nine Oscar nominations and won none.

item.title: For Once, the Oscars Got a Lot Right (Even the In Memoriam Segment)
item.description: The ceremony figured out how to celebrate movies and the people who make them. It even understood Robert Redford’s place in American cinema.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis and Alissa Wilkinson
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Part of the Oscar haul for “One Battle After Another,” which took six trophies.

item.title: Dinner and No Drinks: Restaurants Are Struggling as Americans Drink Less
item.description: Traditionally a reliable revenue stream for restaurants, alcoholic drinks are down markedly — and the bottom line is, too.
item.dc:creator: Meghan McCarron

item.title: In Deep-Red Idaho, a Republican Rift Over Schools and ‘Parental Choice’
item.description: Does “choice” in Idaho mean vouchers for private-school tuition or publicly funded remote learning that has brought AP classes and advanced math to the state’s rural reaches?
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: The town of Carey in rural Blaine County is nestled in a valley in Idaho.

item.title: Kouri Richins Found Guilty of Poisoning Her Husband in Utah Murder Trial
item.description: Kouri Richins was accused of mixing a lethal dose of fentanyl into a drink she made for her husband. She later wrote a children’s book about coping with grief.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:description: Kouri Richins, a Utah mother accused of fatally poisoning her husband, during her murder trial at the Summit County Courthouse in Park City, Utah.

item.title: Is Cheburashka, the Beloved Soviet-Born Character, Ruining Russia?
item.description: Instead of obsessing over the fictional Cheburashka, Russians should be focused on more important things like the rebirth of a Russian empire, influential conservatives say.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan

item.title: Trump’s Dangerous Lack of a Strategy in Iran
item.description: The early reality of the Iran war is not cooperating with President Trump’s bluster.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Illustration by Rebecca Chew/The New York Times

item.title: How Trump Destroyed the Aura of the Wartime President
item.description: Even as he’s wrecking American institutions, he is revealing the limits of his cultural influence.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Has a Bone to Pick With Trump’s War Plans
item.description: “The only war Trump had an exit plan for was Vietnam,” Kimmel remarked after the president said he would end the war in Iran when he “feels it in his bones.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: University of Florida Penalizes GOP Group Over Antisemitism Accusations
item.description: After the University of Florida restricted the Republican organization from operating on campus, the group sued the university arguing its First Amendment rights were violated.
item.dc:creator: Dana Goldstein
item.media:credit: Jacob Langston for The New York Times
item.media:description: The University of Florida’s interim president, Donald W. Landry, said the school supports its Jewish students.

item.title: Colorado Funeral Home Owner Is Sentenced to 18 Years on Federal Fraud Charges
item.description: Carie Hallford, 49, and her husband failed to provide cremation and burial services they had promised to grieving families, prosecutors said.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.category: Return to Nature Funeral Home (HallfordHomes LLC)
item.media:credit: Jerilee Bennett/The Gazette, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The Return to Nature Funeral Home in Penrose, Colo., about 100 miles south of Denver.

item.title: Judy Pace, 83, Dies; Actress Brought Layers to Black Characters
item.description: On the prime-time soap opera “Peyton Place,” she played one of TV’s first Black female antagonists. She was also a fixture in blaxploitation films.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: ABC Photo Archives/Disney General Entertainment Content, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Judy Pace in 1971, when she appeared in “Brian’s Song,” the critically acclaimed, hugely popular TV movie.

item.title: 4 Are Dead, Including a Child, in a Fast-Moving Queens Fire
item.description: The building in Flushing was engulfed in flames by the time firefighters arrived four minutes after a 911 call. Twelve people were injured, at least one critically.
item.dc:creator: Andy Newman, Ellen Yan and Ashley Southall
item.media:credit: Jackie Molloy for The New York Times
